summary: Gruber clarifies his view on Apple's approach to mass-market appeal, arguing it's not about ignoring technology but about priorities. While every phone maker targets the 99% of consumers, most rely on tech specs to compete. Apple's differentiation is making a great product first, then marketing it — not the reverse. The core distinction is that Apple defines product quality by the user experience, not by component specifications.
key_points: Every mass-market phone maker targets the same 99% of consumers; the difference is strategy, not audience
The tech industry's default approach to consumer appeal is specs — gigahertz, megapixels, megabits-per-second
People who attribute Apple's success to 'marketing' misunderstand what good marketing actually is
Apple's formula is sequential: make a great product first, then let people know about it
Product greatness is defined by the total experience of using it, not by technical specifications
money_quotes: The question is how? How do you appeal to the sweet spot of the market where most of the money is and most of the people are?
Almost every other company than Apple is doing this the same way the computer industry always has: tech specs.
This is why so many people attribute Apple's success to 'marketing' as an explanation of how their products can lead in sales but not in specs.
But they misunderstand what good marketing really is.
Here's how Apple does marketing in a nutshell: Make a great product, then let people know about it.
Neither aspect of that is easy, but the important thing is it has to happen in that order. It all starts with a great product.
A great product is defined by the experience of actually using it — not by its individual components' technical specifications.
key_insight: Apple's marketing advantage isn't persuasion — it's the discipline of building a great user experience first, then simply showing people what it is.
